metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the Approach assertion: `InboundMapper` derives from `inbound_fields._map_jira_to_local_fields` (the pure payload→local function in `inbound_fields.py:141`) — `inbound_translate` is the I/O applier-path module and is NOT a source for the InboundMapper Protocol surface.
evidence: inbound_translate.py contains: `_write_event_file`, `_jira_key_to_local_id`, `_read_latest_status`, `_jira_status_to_local` — none of which are payload→local field mappers
grep of inbound_translate for `_map_jira_to_local_fields` returns zero hits
`inbound_fields.py:141` is the sole definition of `_map_jira_to_local_fields`
inbound_translate.py docstring: 'Pure translation + local-event-store IO helpers used by the inbound leaf appliers' — it has I/O, not pure mapping
inbound_differ.py imports `_map_jira_to_local_fields` from `inbound_fields`, not from `inbound_translate`
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'InboundMapper ← the `inbound_fields`/`inbound_translate` payload→local extraction', implying both modules are sources for InboundMapper's Protocol surface. In reality, `inbound_translate` does NOT contain `_map_jira_to_local_fields` or any pure payload→local extraction function — it is the applier-path I/O module (`_write_event_file`, `_jira_key_to_local_id`, `_read_latest_status`, `_jira_status_to_local`). The actual payload→local extraction lives exclusively in `inbound_fields._map_jira_to_local_fields`. Including `inbound_translate` in InboundMapper's definition is an inaccurate codebase assertion that could cause implementors to include I/O or applier-path methods in the InboundMapper Protocol surface.
findings.location: Approach section — 'InboundMapper ← the `inbound_fields`/`inbound_translate` payload→local extraction'
scenarios: An implementor reads the Approach and concludes InboundMapper.Protocol should include methods sourced from inbound_translate (e.g. a `write_event` or `resolve_local_id` method), pulling I/O into what should be a pure mapping Protocol
The JiraBackend wraps both modules' surfaces under InboundMapper, creating a Protocol contract that no pure second-backend mapper can satisfy without local-event-store I/O
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'InboundMapper ← the `inbound_fields`/`inbound_translate` payload→local extraction' to 'InboundMapper ← `inbound_fields._map_jira_to_local_fields` (pure Jira-fields-dict → local-field-dict, in `inbound_fields.py:141`). `inbound_translate` is the applier-path I/O module and is out of scope for this Protocol.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the Approach: `TicketTransport`'s transition method derives from `AcliClient.transition_issue_by_name` (the instance method at `acli.py:481`), not from the module-level `transition_issue` free function at `acli.py:122` — specify the exact method name to prevent a mismatched Protocol definition.
evidence: `acli.py:481`: `def transition_issue_by_name(self, jira_key: str, target_status: str) -> None` — the AcliClient instance method
`acli.py:122`: `def transition_issue(jira_key: str, status: str) -> dict[str, Any]` — a module-level free function that builds its own client; NOT an AcliClient instance method

AcliClient docstring (acli.py:218-221) lists: `create_issue, update_issue, delete_issue, get_issue, search_issues, get_myself, get_server_info, get_comments, set_relationship` — no `transition_issue` in this list
dispatch_one.py and batch_dispatch.py call `client.transition_issue_by_name(...)`, confirming the instance method name
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'TicketTransport ← the create/read/update/transition/comment methods on `acli.AcliClient`', but `transition_issue` on `AcliClient` is actually named `transition_issue_by_name` (at `acli.py:481`), while `transition_issue` (at `acli.py:122`) is a module-level free function that constructs its own `AcliClient` internally. These are two distinct shapes with different signatures. A Protocol derived from 'the transition method on AcliClient' without identifying the correct method name (`transition_issue_by_name`) risks producing a Protocol that neither mirrors the actual instance method nor can serve as a delegation target.
findings.location: Approach section — 'TicketTransport ← the create/read/update/transition/comment methods on `acli.AcliClient`'
scenarios: Implementor defines `TicketTransport.transition_issue(self, jira_key, status)` matching the module-level free function signature, then `JiraBackend` delegates to the wrong callable (the free function that builds its own client instead of the instance method)
The contract test verifies `transition_issue` is callable on the transport object, but `AcliClient` has no such method — the `isinstance` check or duck-typing call fails at runtime
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'transition' in the TicketTransport bullet to 'transition_issue_by_name (AcliClient instance method, acli.py:481)' and add a note that the module-level `acli.transition_issue` free function is out of scope (it constructs its own client internally).

findings.finding: The plan attributes `adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit` to `OutboundMapper` ('OutboundMapper ← outbound_fields._map_local_to_jira_fields (+ adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit)'), but a full read of `_map_local_to_jira_fields` (outbound_fields.py:112-198) and a search of outbound_fields.py for 'fit_text_to_adf' confirm the function is never called there. `fit_text_to_adf_limit` is called from `_diff_fields` (outbound_fields.py:567) — the field-comparison path, not the mapping path. Attributing it to OutboundMapper is a false premise: the Protocol's method surface derived from the de-facto call sites would not include `fit_text_to_adf_limit` under the mapping role.
findings.location: Approach section — 'OutboundMapper ← outbound_fields._map_local_to_jira_fields (+ adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit)'
findings.suggested_fix: Remove `(+ adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit)` from the `OutboundMapper` derivation in the Approach section. If ADF truncation must be part of the backend interface, assign it to the role Protocol whose de-facto surface includes `_diff_fields` (likely a combined OutboundMapper that wraps both map + diff, or a separate FieldNormalizer role), and update the contract test assertion accordingly.

findings.finding: The plan places `adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit` under `OutboundMapper` (`OutboundMapper ← outbound_fields._map_local_to_jira_fields (+ adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit)`), but the actual codebase shows `fit_text_to_adf_limit` is NOT called inside `_map_local_to_jira_fields` — it is called from `_diff_fields` (the differ/comparison path at `outbound_fields.py:567`) and from `inbound_differ.py:192`. The plan's own stated derivation principle is to 'derive each Protocol's method signatures from the de-facto surface the core already calls', which would place `fit_text_to_adf_limit` in `FieldSanitizer` (already backed by `_sanitize_description`, which itself calls `fit_text_to_adf_limit`), not in `OutboundMapper`. Misattributing it to `OutboundMapper` would produce a Protocol that doesn't match actual call-site boundaries, complicating the S4 rewiring where sites call `fit_text_to_adf_limit` via the FieldSanitizer path (description send-side truncation) rather than the mapper path.
findings.location: Approach section — 'OutboundMapper ← outbound_fields._map_local_to_jira_fields (+ adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the Approach bullet to: `OutboundMapper ← outbound_fields._map_local_to_jira_fields` (remove `+ adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit`); add `fit_text_to_adf_limit` to `FieldSanitizer` alongside `_sanitize_description` (which already delegates to it), consistent with `adapters/jira/jira_fields.py:18` importing it for that purpose.

findings.finding: The plan asserts `TicketTransport` is derived from 'the create/read/update/transition/comment methods on `acli.AcliClient`', but `add_label` — used at all three identity-write sites the plan itself cites (dispatch_one.py:320, binding_store.py:516, apply_inbound_records.py:223) — is not listed in the `TicketTransport` method enumeration. `add_label` lives on `AcliGraphMixin` (acli_graph.py:77), which `AcliClient` inherits, but if it is absent from the Protocol, `JiraBackend`'s `IdentityConvention` object cannot issue the label write via the transport, and the contract suite cannot verify delegation of that call.
findings.location: Approach section — 'TicketTransport ← the create/read/update/transition/comment methods on `acli.AcliClient`'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach bullet for TicketTransport, extend the method list: 'TicketTransport ← the create/read/update/transition/comment **and `add_label`** methods on `acli.AcliClient` / `AcliGraphMixin`'. Also add `set_entity_property` (used alongside `add_label` at all three identity-write sites) to the enumeration.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether `IdentityConvention.parse()` must accept both the canonical `rebar-id:{id}` colon form AND the legacy `rebar-id-{id}` hyphen form, and add a contract test assertion for the hyphen-form parse path.
evidence: binding_walk.py:341: for prefix in ("rebar-id:", "rebar-id-"): — both forms scanned
binding_store.py:495: 'falling back to the legacy rebar-id-{local_id} hyphen' — hyphen form is a live code path
Plan: 'formats a RemoteRef → the rebar-id:{remote_id} label string and parses that label back to a RemoteRef' — only colon form mentioned
S4 (6673-7636-a116-4f90) routes binding_walk through IdentityConvention — if parse() is colon-only, the hyphen fallback in binding_walk.py:341 breaks
findings.finding: The `IdentityConvention` Protocol's parse direction is under-specified with respect to the legacy hyphen form. The plan states it 'parses that label back to a RemoteRef' covering only the canonical `rebar-id:{remote_id}` colon form. However, `binding_walk.py:341` (the read site the plan cites) scans for BOTH `rebar-id:` and `rebar-id-` prefixes. If S4 routes `binding_walk.py` through `IdentityConvention.parse()` as intended, a parse method that only handles the colon form will silently fail to recognize legacy hyphen-form labels (`rebar-id-<local_id>`), breaking the L10 guard for any Jira issues that still carry the legacy label. The Protocol method signature in `_backend.py` and the contract test in S2 should pin whether `parse()` is expected to accept both forms.
findings.location: Approach section ('formats a RemoteRef → the rebar-id:{remote_id} label string and parses that label back to a RemoteRef'); Testing section ('IdentityConvention stamps and finds a RemoteRef')
scenarios: An implementer writes IdentityConvention.parse() to handle only 'rebar-id:' prefix. S4 routes binding_walk.py through it. Any Jira issue with a legacy 'rebar-id-' label is no longer recognized as bound — triggers phantom re-adoption or L11 double-bind errors.
Contract test in S2 only stamps/parses the colon form, so the gap is invisible until S4 integration.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether `IdentityConvention.parse()` must recognize both the canonical colon form (`rebar-id:<id>`) and the legacy hyphen form (`rebar-id-<id>`), or is intentionally colon-only with an explicit statement that the hyphen-form scanning in `binding_walk.py:341`, `binding_store.py:526`, and `apply_inbound.py:444` will NOT be routed through `IdentityConvention` in S4 — so S4 implementers know which sites to route and which to leave raw.
evidence: Plan says: 'formats a RemoteRef → the rebar-id:{remote_id} label string and parses that label back to a RemoteRef'
Plan says contract test: 'stamp a RemoteRef → label string; parse label string → RemoteRef; round-trip'
Codebase: binding_walk.py:341 — 'for prefix in ("rebar-id:", "rebar-id-")'
Codebase: binding_store.py:526 — 'hyphen_label = f"rebar-id-{local_id}"' as legacy fallback
Codebase: apply_inbound.py:444 — 'client.remove_label(target, f"rebar-id-{local_id}")'
Plan explicitly out-of-scopes the four call-site rewires to S4, but IdentityConvention is the object S4 will route them through
findings.finding: The IdentityConvention.parse() method is underspecified with respect to the legacy 'rebar-id-' hyphen form. The plan describes parse() as converting a label string back to a RemoteRef and exercising a round-trip in the contract test, but the actual codebase has a well-documented dual-form identity: canonical colon ('rebar-id:') AND legacy hyphen ('rebar-id-'). The hyphen form is load-bearing in binding_walk.py:341 (_has_rebar_id_label checks both prefixes), binding_store.py:526 (hyphen-form JQL fallback search), differ.py:92/498, inbound_differ.py:251, and apply_inbound.py:324/444. When S4 routes those four core call sites through IdentityConvention, the implementer needs to know whether parse() must also recognize 'rebar-id-<id>' or whether IdentityConvention is intentionally colon-only and the hyphen-form scanning stays raw at those sites. Without this specification, the S4 implementer could silently break legacy hyphen-form binding recovery by routing binding_walk and binding_store through a colon-only parse().
findings.location: Approach section — IdentityConvention description; also binding_walk.py:341, binding_store.py:526
scenarios: S4 routes binding_walk.py:341 and binding_store.py:526 through IdentityConvention.parse(); if parse() only handles 'rebar-id:' prefix, legacy hyphen-form issues (pre-colon-migration Jira tickets) are no longer recognized as identity-bound, causing them to be double-adopted or mis-recovered on the next reconciler pass.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the IdentityConvention approach: 'IdentityConvention.parse() recognizes both the canonical colon form ("rebar-id:<id>") and the legacy hyphen form ("rebar-id-<id>") when parsing a label to a RemoteRef, matching the dual-prefix logic currently in binding_walk.py:341 and binding_store.py:495-526, so S4 can safely route those sites through the convention object.' Alternatively, if colon-only is intentional, explicitly state that binding_walk and binding_store's hyphen-scanning paths are out of scope for S4's IdentityConvention rewire.

findings.finding: The IdentityConvention object as specified covers only the canonical colon-form (rebar-id:{remote_id} stamp and parse), but the live identity convention the codebase actually enforces is dual-form: colon ('rebar-id:') for canonical writes and legacy hyphen ('rebar-id-') for backwards-compatible reads/recovery. The dual-form is active in at least 8 core sites not listed in the plan (differ.py:91-92, outbound_differ.py:302, inbound_differ.py:251, inbound_translate.py:76-77, rebar_id_audit.py multiple sites, reconcile_check.py:389/403, apply_inbound.py:324, binding_store.py:526), all of which scan for BOTH prefixes. The IdentityConvention contract test as described ('stamp a RemoteRef → label string; parse label string → RemoteRef; round-trip') tests only the colon path. If S4 routes callers through this object as a complete single source of truth, callers expecting a parse-both-forms interface will be surprised — or the object will silently be incomplete and the legacy-form logic stays inline everywhere else.
findings.location: Approach section — IdentityConvention definition; Testing section — 'IdentityConvention stamps and finds a RemoteRef'
findings.suggested_fix: In the IdentityConvention spec under Approach, add a sentence: 'The convention object exposes two methods: stamp(ref: RemoteRef) → str (colon-form only) and parse(label: str) → RemoteRef | None (recognises both colon and legacy hyphen forms, returning None for non-matching strings). The legacy hyphen-form (rebar-id-{local_id}) is read-only recognised for backwards compatibility with pre-migration Jira issues; new writes always use the colon form.' Update the contract test description to include a parse(hyphen-form-label) → RemoteRef assertion.

findings.finding: IdentityConvention is described as a 'new, self-contained pure convention object' with 'no single existing function to delegate to', but the plan never specifies its method names or signatures. The Testing section says the contract test exercises 'stamp a RemoteRef → label string; parse label string → RemoteRef; round-trip', implying at least two methods (format/stamp and parse), but neither is named. Furthermore, binding_walk.py:341 shows the codebase currently tolerates BOTH 'rebar-id:' (colon) and 'rebar-id-' (hyphen) prefix forms in parse/scan operations, yet the plan only specifies the colon format. Whether IdentityConvention.parse must also handle the hyphen legacy form to be contract-correct is unresolved.
findings.location: Approach section — 'IdentityConvention' bullet; Testing section — 'IdentityConvention stamps and finds a RemoteRef'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Approach section: 'IdentityConvention protocol: label_for(ref: RemoteRef) -> str — returns "rebar-id:{ref.remote_id}"; parse_label(label: str) -> RemoteRef | None — matches both "rebar-id:" (canonical) and "rebar-id-" (legacy tolerance, mirroring binding_walk.py:341) prefixes and returns a RemoteRef on match or None on no-match. Round-trip contract: parse_label(label_for(ref)) == ref.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the IdentityConvention spec (adapters/jira/identity.py design) whether parse() must accept the legacy 'rebar-id-' hyphen form in addition to the canonical 'rebar-id:' colon form, and add a contract test case for legacy-label parsing if it must.
evidence: Plan: 'parses label back to a RemoteRef (the single source of the format those four sites currently hand-inline)'
binding_walk.py:341: 'for prefix in ("rebar-id:", "rebar-id-"): if lbl.startswith(prefix) and lbl[len(prefix):].strip(): return True' — live code handles both forms
binding_store.py:526: 'hyphen_label = f"rebar-id-{local_id}"' — legacy fallback explicitly exists
Plan: 'stamp a RemoteRef → label string; parse label string → RemoteRef; round-trip' — the round-trip spec does not mention the hyphen form
Contract test section: 'IdentityConvention stamps and finds a RemoteRef' — ambiguous whether 'finds' includes legacy-hyphen parsing
findings.finding: The IdentityConvention format rule is specified as 'stamps a RemoteRef → rebar-id:{remote_id} label string and parses that label back to a RemoteRef', but the plan never addresses whether parse() must also handle the legacy hyphen form 'rebar-id-{remote_id}' that currently exists in binding_walk.py:341 and binding_store.py:526. This is a decision rule co-located neither with the IdentityConvention schema nor with the contract test spec.
findings.location: Approach section: IdentityConvention / adapters/jira/identity.py
scenarios: Implementor omits hyphen-form parsing from IdentityConvention.parse(), causing binding_walk.py (S4 rewire target) to silently drop pre-migration 'rebar-id-<id>' labels
Contract test only round-trips the colon form, so the regression is undetected until S4 integration
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the IdentityConvention Approach paragraph: 'parse() accepts both the canonical colon form (rebar-id:{id}) and the legacy hyphen form (rebar-id-{id}) for backward compatibility, mirroring binding_walk.py:341.' Then add a contract test case: parse('rebar-id-legacy-id') returns a valid RemoteRef.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the method signature(s) for each of the three capability Protocols (SupportsLinks, SupportsComments, SupportsIncremental) in the plan — at minimum, list the method name(s) each adds so the FakeBackend and contract tests can be authored against a pinned surface.
evidence: 'three opt-in capability Protocols (SupportsLinks, SupportsComments, SupportsIncremental)' — defined by name only
Testing: 'a capability Protocol's methods are exercised only when the backend advertises it' — refers to unspecified methods
AC2: 'the contract suite passes when parameterized over both JiraBackend (injected mock transport) and FakeBackend' — capability methods must be known for FakeBackend to selectively implement them
Grep confirms SupportsLinks, SupportsComments, SupportsIncremental do not yet exist in codebase — they are fully new and thus fully unspecified
findings.finding: The three capability Protocols (SupportsLinks, SupportsComments, SupportsIncremental) are named and their isinstance-detection mechanism is specified, but their method vocabularies are never listed anywhere. The contract suite is required to exercise 'a capability Protocol's methods' — but neither the implementor nor the test author knows what those methods are.
findings.location: Edit-set: _backend.py capability Protocols / Testing section
scenarios: FakeBackend inherits SupportsLinks to exercise the guarded path — but no one knows what method(s) to implement on it
Contract test for SupportsComments cannot be written RED-first without knowing whether the method is add_comment(key,body) vs post_comment(key,body,author)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand the AC1 import test to explicitly name all eight Protocol symbols: `from rebar._engine.rebar_reconciler._backend import Backend, RemoteRef, TicketTransport, OutboundMapper, InboundMapper, FieldSanitizer, IdentityConvention, SupportsLinks, SupportsComments, SupportsIncremental` and assert each name resolves.
evidence: AC1 text: 'defines `Backend`, the five role Protocols, the three capability Protocols (`SupportsLinks`, `SupportsComments`, `SupportsIncremental`), and `RemoteRef`'

AC1 verify: 'verified by an import test that asserts each name resolves and `RemoteRef(vendor=…, instance=…, remote_id=…)` constructs' — but the only import shown is `from rebar._engine.rebar_reconciler._backend import Backend, RemoteRef`

ABSENCE: none of `TicketTransport`, `OutboundMapper`, `InboundMapper`, `FieldSanitizer`, `IdentityConvention`, `SupportsLinks`, `SupportsComments`, `SupportsIncremental` appear in the verify import statement
findings.finding: AC1's verify step only imports two names (`Backend, RemoteRef`) but the criterion body claims to verify that `_backend.py` defines the five role Protocols AND the three capability Protocols too — those eight names are never checked in the stated import test, leaving most of the deliverable unverified by its own acceptance criterion.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1
scenarios: An implementer ships `_backend.py` missing `SupportsLinks` or `OutboundMapper`; AC1's import test still passes because it only checks `Backend` and `RemoteRef`, so the gap goes undetected until AC2's contract suite (if it imports them explicitly).
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the AC1 verify clause with: 'verified by an import test that executes `from rebar._engine.rebar_reconciler._backend import Backend, RemoteRef, TicketTransport, OutboundMapper, InboundMapper, FieldSanitizer, IdentityConvention, SupportsLinks, SupportsComments, SupportsIncremental` and asserts each name is non-None, `RemoteRef(vendor=…, instance=…, remote_id=…)` constructs, and each Protocol passes `runtime_checkable` isinstance checks on a trivially conforming object.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit baseline-capture step (e.g. `git stash && python scripts/capture_mapper_fixtures.py > tests/fixtures/mapper_baseline.json && git stash pop`) to AC3 so the executor captures the snapshot from unmodified `main` before implementing `JiraBackend`.
evidence: 'verified by literal expected values captured from current behavior (a diff of the captured vs. produced dict is empty)'
ABSENCE: no 'run this command against main to produce fixtures/expected_*.json' step appears in Testing or Approach
findings.finding: AC3 requires 'literal expected values captured from current behavior' to anchor the characterization tests, but the plan provides no command or step for capturing that baseline snapshot from pre-story `main`. An executing agent must infer the process (check out main, run the pure-mapper functions, serialize outputs, commit as fixture), which could produce divergent baselines if done inconsistently.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 ('Characterization tests assert the pure Jira mapper outputs are byte-for-byte identical to pre-story main…')
scenarios: Agent captures baseline on a branch that already includes partial changes, invalidating the oracle.
Two agents capture baseline at different commits, producing non-reproducible fixtures.
findings.suggested_fix: In AC3, append: 'Baseline captured by running `python tests/scripts/capture_mapper_baseline.py` on unmodified `main` before any S2 edits; the resulting `tests/fixtures/mapper_baseline.json` is committed as the oracle.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the test file path: update `file_impact` to `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/test_backend_contract.py` to match the plan body, AC2, and AC5.
evidence: Plan body: 'New tests: tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/test_backend_contract.py'
file_impact in ticket record: {"path": "tests/reconciler/test_backend_contract.py", ...}
Directory tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/ exists; tests/reconciler/ does NOT exist (list_directory confirmed)
AC5: 'pytest tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/test_backend_contract.py'
findings.finding: The test file path is inconsistently specified: the plan body names `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/test_backend_contract.py` (consistent with the existing test layout), but the ticket's `file_impact` metadata records `tests/reconciler/test_backend_contract.py` — a directory that does not exist in the repo. The AC5 (Teeth) and AC4 git-diff whitelist also reference `tests/` loosely, but AC2 and the Testing section both cite the `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/` path. An implementer following file_impact will create a file in a non-existent directory.
findings.location: Edit-set (plan body) vs. file_impact metadata; AC2 (`pytest tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/test_backend_contract.py`); AC5 (`pytest tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/test_backend_contract.py`)
scenarios: Implementer follows file_impact metadata and places the contract test at tests/reconciler/test_backend_contract.py — pytest discovery misses it or the directory must be created, diverging from the existing test layout.
AC5's verify command (pytest tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/test_backend_contract.py) points to a different path than file_impact, so the AC self-check would fail if file_impact was followed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'byte-for-byte identical' in AC3 with a normalization-anchored assertion (e.g. `assert actual == expected` on the raw Python dicts, or JSON-serialized with `sort_keys=True`), and specify that the captured fixture is stored as a Python dict literal or sorted JSON so the comparison is deterministic.
evidence: 'verified by literal expected values captured from current behavior (a diff of the captured vs. produced dict is empty)'
ABSENCE: no normalization/sort step is specified for the dict comparison
findings.finding: AC3 asserts 'byte-for-byte identical' outputs for Python dict-valued mapper functions but does not specify the comparison mechanism. Python dicts are unordered in serialization; a naive `repr()` or JSON dump without `sort_keys=True` can produce spurious mismatches or mask real ones, making the criterion not reliably measurable as written.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 ('byte-for-byte identical … a diff of the captured vs. produced dict is empty')
scenarios: Test passes even when a value changes because key ordering differences mask the change in the diff.
Test spuriously fails on a Python version where dict repr ordering differs.
findings.suggested_fix: In AC3, replace 'a diff of the captured vs. produced dict is empty' with 'pytest asserts `actual_dict == expected_dict` (Python structural equality) with the expected dict stored as a Python literal in the test file, so comparison is key-order-independent and deterministic'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add (or update) an ADR / AGENTS.md section capturing the Backend facade contract: the five role Protocols and their method-signature rationale, the three opt-in capability Protocols with the `isinstance`/`@runtime_checkable` detection idiom (and explicit 'no `capabilities()` method' decision), the `RemoteRef` fields, and the canonical `IdentityConvention` label format — so a second-backend author has a single authoritative reference.
evidence: 'no named interface a second backend could implement' — confirms the Backend Protocol suite is genuinely new
'RemoteRef{vendor, instance, remote_id} dataclass pinned in ADR 0035 §(d)' — only RemoteRef is mentioned as pinned; the Backend facade and Protocol hierarchy are not
'There is no capabilities() method' — a deliberate design choice not recorded in any named decision artifact
ABSENCE: no mention of 'update ADR 0035', 'new ADR', or 'design doc' anywhere in the plan
The IdentityConvention label format (rebar-id:{remote_id}) is newly canonicalized here but no ADR entry is mentioned
findings.finding: The plan introduces the Backend facade, five role Protocols, three opt-in capability Protocols, and the `IdentityConvention` format as new architectural decisions, but does not mention updating ADR 0035 or creating a new ADR/design-doc to capture these contracts. ADR 0035 §(d) is cited only for `RemoteRef`; the Protocol hierarchy, method signatures, and capability-detection mechanism (no `capabilities()` method; pure `isinstance`-based dispatch; `@runtime_checkable`) are all novel decisions first established in this story and left undocumented at the ADR level.
findings.location: Context/Problem (ADR 0035 reference), Approach (capability-detection mechanism, IdentityConvention), Scope, AC1
scenarios: A future implementor adding a second backend cannot find the authoritative contract spec and must read source code to infer the Protocol rules and capability-detection idiom
The capability-detection convention (`isinstance` with `@runtime_checkable` Protocols, no `capabilities()` method) diverges in a future story because the rationale was never recorded
The IdentityConvention label format is independently re-derived differently in another adapter because the format spec lives only in code
findings.suggested_fix: Either add a new ADR (e.g. `docs/adr/0036-backend-facade-protocol.md`) or extend ADR 0035 with a §(e) that records: (1) the Backend Protocol hierarchy and role of each Protocol; (2) the capability-detection idiom (`@runtime_checkable` + `isinstance`, rationale for no `capabilities()` method); (3) the `IdentityConvention` label format and its canonical home. Reference this ADR in `_backend.py`'s module docstring.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the 'byte-for-byte identical to pre-story main' self-authored-oracle characterization tests with tests that assert concrete, hand-authored expected values (e.g. expected field dict constructed from the Jira API spec), or explain why the captured snapshots are known-correct — otherwise the suite locks in latent mapper bugs.
evidence: 'Characterization tests assert the pure Jira mapper outputs are byte-for-byte identical to pre-story main for the representative fixtures'
'verified by literal expected values captured from current behavior (a diff of the captured vs. produced dict is empty)'
E5 rubric: 'snapshot-of-current-output, tautology, or source-grep masquerading as a behavioral test (MAJOR — locks in the bug)'
findings.finding: The characterization tests are described as asserting 'byte-for-byte identical to pre-story main' outputs using 'literal expected values captured from current behavior' — the textbook self-authored-oracle / change-detector anti-pattern. If any current mapper has a latent bug, these tests will bless and lock in that bug; they test only 'same as before,' not correctness.
findings.location: Testing section — 'Characterization tests pin the exact current outputs… byte-for-byte identical to pre-story main… a diff of the captured vs. produced dict is empty'; AC3.
scenarios: A mapper produces a subtly wrong field (e.g. priority mapped to wrong Jira value); the characterization test captures and asserts the wrong value; subsequent stories treat the snapshot as ground truth and never catch the defect.
A future refactor that fixes a mapper bug causes the characterization test to go RED, misleading the author into thinking they broke something rather than fixed it.
findings.suggested_fix: For each representative fixture, hand-author the expected output dict from the Jira field spec (e.g. 'priority field maps to {"name": "High"} for a local priority of HIGH') rather than capturing current output. This turns a change-detector into a behavioral assertion and lets the test catch both delegation regressions AND pre-existing mapper bugs.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a method-signature table (or stub list) to the `_backend.py` edit-set spec for each of the five role Protocols, naming every method with its parameter list and return type — so the contract test author can write RED tests without reading `acli.py`, `outbound_fields.py`, or `inbound_fields.py`.
evidence: 'TicketTransport ← the create/read/update/transition/comment methods on acli.AcliClient' — method names are never enumerated
'OutboundMapper ← outbound_fields._map_local_to_jira_fields (+ adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit)' — signature not given
'InboundMapper ← the inbound_fields/inbound_translate payload→local extraction' — no method name or signature listed
'FieldSanitizer ← adapters/jira/jira_fields.py sanitizers + comment_limits size-fit' — no method name listed

Testing section: 'the contract suite is parameterized over a backend_factory fixture and run against both JiraBackend and FakeBackend' — the suite author must know method signatures to write RED-first tests
AC2: 'the contract suite passes when parameterized over both JiraBackend and FakeBackend' — verifiable only if method signatures are pinned
findings.finding: The five role Protocols (TicketTransport, OutboundMapper, InboundMapper, FieldSanitizer, IdentityConvention) are named throughout as the central schema of _backend.py, but their method signatures are never enumerated anywhere in the plan. The Approach says 'Derive each Protocol's method signatures from the de-facto surface the core already calls' — this is a derivation instruction to the implementor, not a specification. The contract test suite must be authored RED-first against specific method calls, so the method vocabulary is load-bearing for the test author.
findings.location: Approach section / Edit-set: _backend.py
scenarios: Implementor A derives `TicketTransport.create_issue(fields)` while test author B derives `TicketTransport.create(fields, project)` — RED-first test passes for wrong reasons on one impl and fails on the other
FakeBackend author cannot know which methods to implement without reading five separate source files
findings.suggested_fix: In the _backend.py edit-set block, add a compact table, e.g.: TicketTransport: create_issue(fields)->str, get_issue(key)->dict, update_issue(key,fields)->None, transition_issue(key,transition_id)->None, add_comment(key,body)->None; OutboundMapper: map_outbound(ticket)->dict[str,Any]; InboundMapper: map_inbound(fields)->dict[str,Any]; FieldSanitizer: sanitize(fields)->dict[str,Any]; IdentityConvention: format_label(ref:RemoteRef)->str, parse_label(lbl:str)->RemoteRef|None.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state the field types for RemoteRef (e.g., `vendor: str, instance: str, remote_id: str`) in the _backend.py edit-set spec so the import test and S4 construction sites agree without inference.
evidence: Plan: 'the RemoteRef{vendor, instance, remote_id} dataclass' — no field types stated
AC1: 'RemoteRef(vendor=…, instance=…, remote_id=…) constructs' — type of each field not pinned

S4 ticket (6673-7636-a116-4f90): 'Replace apply_inbound_records.py:70,74 jira literals with RemoteRef/IdentityConvention' — a RemoteRef must be constructed from env-derived strings, so str typing is implied but not stated
findings.finding: RemoteRef's field types are not specified. The plan lists the field names ({vendor, instance, remote_id}) but never states their types. AC1 requires an import test that constructs RemoteRef(vendor=…, instance=…, remote_id=…), and S4 (rebar:6673-7636-a116-4f90) replaces hardcoded 'jira' literals with RemoteRef — making the type contract load-bearing for downstream stories.
findings.location: Edit-set: _backend.py / AC1
scenarios: One implementor uses str for all fields; another uses an Enum for vendor — S4 construction sites break
Typecheck (mypy/pyright) fails if the field types are inferred inconsistently across the two stories
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the RemoteRef description: 'RemoteRef(vendor: str, instance: str, remote_id: str) — all fields are plain strings; vendor is the backend identifier (e.g. "jira"), instance is the base URL or logical name, remote_id is the vendor-assigned ticket key.'

findings.finding: AC4's `git diff --name-only` whitelist names only `_backend.py`, `adapters/jira/backend.py`, and `tests/` — but the Approach section explicitly introduces `IdentityConvention` as a 'new, self-contained pure convention object under `adapters/jira/`' that is distinct from `backend.py`. A correct implementation would produce an additional file (e.g. `adapters/jira/identity_convention.py`) that is not on AC4's list, causing the AC to fail on a valid implementation.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4; Approach section — IdentityConvention paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC4 to: 'verified by `git diff --name-only` touching only `_backend.py`, `adapters/jira/backend.py`, `adapters/jira/identity_convention.py` (or the chosen name for the IdentityConvention object), and files under `tests/`.'

findings.finding: The `IdentityConvention` object is described in Approach as 'a new, self-contained pure convention object under `adapters/jira/`' but the plan's Edit-set only enumerates two new files: `_backend.py` and `adapters/jira/backend.py`. If `IdentityConvention` is co-located inside `adapters/jira/backend.py` this is fine, but the plan never pins that co-location — it might land in its own third file (e.g., `adapters/jira/identity_convention.py`). AC #4's git-diff verification clause names only `_backend.py` and `adapters/jira/backend.py`, which would falsify if a third file is created.
findings.location: Edit-set section; Approach ('IdentityConvention' paragraph); AC #4
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Edit-set and Approach: 'IdentityConvention is defined inside `adapters/jira/backend.py` (co-located with JiraBackend)' — or if it warrants its own file, add `adapters/jira/identity_convention.py` to the Edit-set and the AC #4 git-diff check.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
